content: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is a supraventricular tachyarrhythmia characterized by uncoordinated atrial activation and ineffective atrial contraction. ECG findings: irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, variable R-R intervals, fibrillatory waves. Classification: paroxysmal (terminates spontaneously or with intervention within 7 days), persistent (>7 days or requiring cardioversion), long-standing persistent (>12 months), permanent (accepted by patient and clinician). Pathophysiology involves triggers (often from pulmonary veins) and substrate (atrial remodeling, fibrosis, dilation). Risk factors: age, hypertension, heart failure, valvular disease (especially mitral), obesity, sleep apnea, diabetes, hyperthyroidism, alcohol ('holiday heart'), genetics. AF causes loss of atrial kick (reduces cardiac output 10-30%), rapid ventricular response (causes symptoms and tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy), and stasis leading to thrombus formation (stroke risk 5-fold increase).
